title: Galette bretonne
subtitle: Breton large-format butter biscuit cake
excerpt: Galette bretonne is a thick, round Breton biscuit baked as a single large cake: heavily buttered, slightly sandy in texture, and scored with a knife in a crosshatch pattern before baking. It is a sablé Breton scaled up to cake size, so it keeps better than individual biscuits and cuts into wedges at the table. The flavour is almost entirely butter and salt; the salt quantity is deliberately generous. It is one of those things that improves with standing.
sourceNotes: Galette bretonne belongs to the wider tradition of Breton butter biscuit baking that includes sablé Breton and kouign-amann. Brittany's semi-salted butter (beurre demi-sel) is the essential ingredient; the same butter that defines the region's cooking. The large-format galette is associated with bakeries in Finistère and the Morbihan department. Recipes appear in French regional baking guides from the early twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Galette bretonne improves overnight. Store at room temperature in an airtight container for up to 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; lower temperature produces an even, deep gold without burning the butter

content.text: The dough is mixed by hand and pressed directly into the tin rather than rolled; it is too rich and short to roll cleanly. The crosshatch scoring is traditional and breaks the crust as it bakes, which helps the butter-heavy base cook through without pooling. The galette is done when it is a uniform deep amber on top: pale means undercooked. It sets as it cools; the centre will seem slightly soft when hot.

items.prepNote: or use salted butter and omit this
beaten with 1 tsp water, for egg wash

content.text: Beat 180 g softened butter with 120 g caster sugar until pale and creamy. Beat in 3 egg yolks, 1 tsp salt, and 0.5 tsp vanilla. Add 200 g flour and mix until the dough just comes together (it will be soft and slightly sticky. Do not overwork it. Pat into a flat disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 60 minutes. Heat the oven to 165 degrees C fan. Grease a 20 cm round cake tin. Press the chilled dough evenly into the tin to a depth of about 1.5 cm. Brush the top with the egg yolk wash. Using the back of a fork or a knife, score a crosshatch pattern across the surface. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until uniformly deep amber. Cool completely in the tin before turning out) it is fragile when warm.

items.problem: Centre is undercooked and doughy after the full bake time
items.solution: The oven temperature was too high. A high temperature browns the surface quickly while the centre stays raw. Bake at 165 degrees C and check at 30 minutes — the internal centre should reach about 95 degrees C. A skewer should come out clean.

items.problem: Galette crumbled when turned out of the tin
items.solution: It was turned out while still warm. The high butter content means it is very fragile hot. Cool completely in the tin — at least 30 minutes — before turning onto a rack.

items.problem: Dough stuck to the tin and came out in pieces
items.solution: The tin was not greased thoroughly, or the galette was turned out too soon. Line the base with parchment in addition to greasing the sides. Wait until fully cold.
